From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [County constituency](/w/index.php?title=County_constituency&redirect=no "County constituency"))

Various types of electoral area in the UK

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/UK_Constituencies_2024_%28blank%29.svg/250px-UK_Constituencies_2024_%28blank%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UK_Constituencies_2024_(blank).svg)

There are 650 constituencies for the UK House of Commons.

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") (UK), each of the electoral areas or divisions called constituencies elects one member to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom").

Within the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") there are five bodies with members elected by electoral districts called "[constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies "Constituencies")" as opposed to "[wards](/wiki/Ward_(electoral_subdivision) "Ward (electoral subdivision)")":

* The [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") (see [Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom"))
* The [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") (see [Scottish Parliament constituencies and regions](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_constituencies_and_regions "Scottish Parliament constituencies and regions"))
* The [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") (see [Senedd constituencies and electoral regions](/wiki/Senedd_constituencies_and_electoral_regions "Senedd constituencies and electoral regions"))
* The [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") (see [Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly_constituencies "Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies"))
* The [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly") (see [List of London Assembly constituencies](/wiki/List_of_London_Assembly_constituencies "List of London Assembly constituencies"))

Between 1921 and 1973 the following body also included members elected by constituencies:

* The [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") (see [Northern Ireland Parliament constituencies](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Parliament_constituencies "Northern Ireland Parliament constituencies"))

Electoral areas called constituencies were previously used in elections to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament"), prior to [the United Kingdom's exit from the European Union](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") (see [European Parliament constituency](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituency "European Parliament constituency")).

In [local government](/wiki/Local_government_(United_Kingdom) "Local government (United Kingdom)") elections (other than for the London Assembly) electoral areas are called [wards](/wiki/Wards_(UK) "Wards (UK)") or [electoral divisions](/wiki/Electoral_division_(UK) "Electoral division (UK)").

## County constituencies and borough constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: County constituencies and borough constituencies")]

House of Commons, Scottish Parliament, Senedd and Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies are designated as either *county* or *borough* constituencies, except that in Scotland the term *[burgh](/wiki/Burgh_constituency "Burgh constituency")* is used instead of *borough*. Since the advent of [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), the differences between county and borough constituencies are slight. Formerly (see [below](#History)) the franchise differed, and there were also *county borough* and *university* constituencies.

Borough constituencies are predominantly [urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") while county constituencies are predominantly [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural"). There is no definitive statutory criterion for the distinction; the Boundary Commission for England has stated that, "as a general principle, where constituencies contain more than a small rural element they should normally be designated as county constituencies. Otherwise they should be designated as borough constituencies."[[1]](#cite_note-bce2007-1) In Scotland, all House of Commons constituencies are county constituencies except those in the cities of [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee") and three urban areas of [Lanarkshire](/wiki/Lanarkshire "Lanarkshire").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In England and Wales, the position of [returning officer](/wiki/Returning_officer "Returning officer") in borough constituencies is held *ex officio* by the mayor or chairman of the borough or district council, and the [high sheriff](/wiki/High_sheriff "High sheriff") of the county in county constituencies.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The administration of elections is carried out by the acting returning officer, who will typically be a local council's [chief executive](/wiki/Chief_executive "Chief executive")[[4]](#cite_note-scc-4) or Head of Legal Services. The role, however, is separate from these posts, and can be held by any person appointed by the council. The spending limits for election campaigns are different in the two, the reasoning being that candidates in county constituencies tend to need to travel farther.

Spending limits for election campaigns

| Elected body | Constituency type | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| borough/burgh | county |
| **House of Commons**[[5]](#cite_note-ropa-1983-s76-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) | £11,390 + 8p per elector | £11,390 + 12p per elector |
| **Northern Ireland Assembly** | £5,483 + 4.6p per elector | £5,483 + 6.2p per elector |
| **Scottish Parliament and Senedd** | £5,761 + 4.8p per elector | £5,761 + 6.5p per elector |

For [by-elections](/wiki/By-election "By-election") to any of these bodies, the limit in all constituencies is £100,000.[[5]](#cite_note-ropa-1983-s76-5)[[7]](#cite_note-ppera-2000-s135-7)

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Unreformed House of Commons](/wiki/Unreformed_House_of_Commons "Unreformed House of Commons") and [Parliamentary franchise in the United Kingdom 1885–1918](/wiki/Parliamentary_franchise_in_the_United_Kingdom_1885%E2%80%931918 "Parliamentary franchise in the United Kingdom 1885–1918")

In the [House of Commons of England](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England"), each [English county](/wiki/English_county "English county") elected two "[knights of the shire](/wiki/Knights_of_the_shire "Knights of the shire")" while each enfranchised [borough](/wiki/Ancient_borough "Ancient borough") elected "burgesses" (usually two, sometimes four, and in a few cases one).[[8]](#cite_note-8) [From 1535](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542") each [Welsh county](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_Wales "Historic counties of Wales") and borough was represented, by one knight or burgess.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The franchise was restricted differently in different types of constituency; in county constituencies [forty shilling freeholders](/wiki/Forty_shilling_freeholders "Forty shilling freeholders") (i.e. landowners) could vote, while in boroughs the franchise varied from [potwallopers](/wiki/Potwalloper "Potwalloper"), giving many residents votes, to [rotten boroughs](/wiki/Rotten_borough "Rotten borough") with hardly any voters.  A *county borough* was the constituency of a [county corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate"), combining the franchises of both county and borough. Until 1950 there were also [university constituencies](/wiki/University_constituencies "University constituencies"), which gave graduates an additional representation.

Similar distinctions applied in the [Irish House of Commons](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Commons "Irish House of Commons"), while the non-university elected members of the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") were called Shire Commissioners and Burgh Commissioners. After the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), Scottish burghs were grouped into [districts of burghs](/wiki/Districts_of_burghs "Districts of burghs") in the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), except that [Edinburgh was a constituency in its own right](/wiki/Edinburgh_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh (UK Parliament constituency)"). After the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), smaller Irish boroughs were disenfranchised, while most others returned only one MP to the United Kingdom Parliament.

The [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") reduced the number of [parliamentary boroughs](/wiki/Parliamentary_borough "Parliamentary borough") in England and Wales by eliminating the rotten boroughs. It also divided larger counties into two two-seat *divisions*, the boundaries of which were defined in the [Parliamentary Boundaries Act 1832](/wiki/Parliamentary_Boundaries_Act_1832 "Parliamentary Boundaries Act 1832"), and gave seven counties a third member. Similar reforms were also made [for Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Reform_Act_1832 "Scottish Reform Act 1832") and [for Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Reform_Act_1832 "Irish Reform Act 1832"). The [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885") ([48 & 49 Vict.](/wiki/48_%26_49_Vict. "48 & 49 Vict.") c. 23) equalised the population of constituencies; it split larger boroughs into multiple single-member constituencies, reduced smaller boroughs from two seats each to one, split each two-seat county and division into two single-member constituencies, and each three-seat county into single-member constituencies.

The [House of Commons (Redistribution of Seats) Act 1958](/wiki/House_of_Commons_(Redistribution_of_Seats)_Act_1958 "House of Commons (Redistribution of Seats) Act 1958") eliminated the previous common electoral quota for the whole United Kingdom and replaced it with four separate national minimal seat quotas for the respective Boundaries commissions to work to. As a result the separate national electoral quotas came into effect: England 69,534; Northern Ireland 67,145, Wales 58,383 and in Scotland only 54,741 electors.

## Naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Naming")]

The [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Act_2011 "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011") gives the Boundary Commissions for [England](/wiki/Boundary_Commission_for_England "Boundary Commission for England"), [Wales](/wiki/Boundary_Commission_for_Wales "Boundary Commission for Wales"), [Scotland](/wiki/Boundary_Commission_for_Scotland "Boundary Commission for Scotland") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Boundary_Commission_for_Northern_Ireland "Boundary Commission for Northern Ireland") the power to create names for constituencies, and does not provide a set of statutory guidelines for the Commissions to follow in doing so.

Constituency names are geographic, and "should normally reflect the main population centre(s) contained in the constituency". [Compass points](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction") are used to distinguish constituencies from each other when a more suitable label cannot be found. Where used, "The compass point reference used will generally form a prefix in cases where the rest of the constituency name refers to the county area or a local council, but a suffix where the rest of the name refers to a population centre." This is the reason for the difference in naming[[10]](#cite_note-10) between, for example, [South Northamptonshire](/wiki/South_Northamptonshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Northamptonshire (UK Parliament constituency)") (a [county constituency](/wiki/County_constituency "County constituency")) and [Northampton South](/wiki/Northampton_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Northampton South (UK Parliament constituency)") (a [borough constituency](/wiki/Borough_constituency "Borough constituency")).

## House of Commons constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: House of Commons constituencies")]

In the [2005 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), the House of Commons had [646 constituencies](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies") covering the whole of the United Kingdom. This rose to 650 in the [2010 election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election") following the [Fifth Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/Fifth_Periodic_Review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Fifth Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies"). Each constituency elects one [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) by the "[first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post")" system of election.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The House of Commons is one of the two chambers of the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), the other being the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)")
* [Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom")
* [List of former United Kingdom Parliament constituencies](/wiki/List_of_former_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies "List of former United Kingdom Parliament constituencies")
* [University constituency](/wiki/University_constituency "University constituency")
* [Number of Westminster MPs](/wiki/Number_of_Westminster_MPs "Number of Westminster MPs")
* [List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies_(2024%E2%80%93present)_by_region "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region")

## London Assembly constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: London Assembly constituencies")]

Main articles: [London Assembly constituencies](/wiki/London_Assembly_constituencies "London Assembly constituencies") and [List of London Assembly constituencies](/wiki/List_of_London_Assembly_constituencies "List of London Assembly constituencies")

There are fourteen [London Assembly constituencies](/wiki/London_Assembly_constituencies "London Assembly constituencies") covering the [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") area, and each constituency elects one member of the assembly by the [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_system "First-past-the-post system"). Eleven [additional members](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system") are elected from Greater London as a whole to produce a form or degree of [mixed-member proportional representation](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation").

Constituency names and boundaries remain now as they were for the [first general election](/wiki/2000_London_Assembly_election "2000 London Assembly election") of the assembly, in 2000.

The assembly is part of the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority") and general elections of the assembly are held at the same time as election of the [mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London").

## Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies")]

Main article: [Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly_constituencies "Northern Ireland Assembly constituencies")

There are 18 [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") [Constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies "Constituencies"): four borough (for [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast")) and 14 county constituencies elsewhere (see below).

Each elects five [MLAs](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_(Northern_Ireland) "Member of the Legislative Assembly (Northern Ireland)") to the 90 member NI Assembly by means of the [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system. Assembly Constituency boundaries are identical to their [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") equivalents.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The constituencies below are not used for the election of members to the 11 [district councils](/wiki/Districts_of_Northern_Ireland "Districts of Northern Ireland").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

| Name | Current boundaries | Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1. [Belfast East BC](/wiki/Belfast_East_(Assembly_constituency) "Belfast East (Assembly constituency)") 2. [Belfast North BC](/wiki/Belfast_North_(Assembly_constituency) "Belfast North (Assembly constituency)") 3. [Belfast South BC](/wiki/Belfast_South_(Assembly_constituency) "Belfast South (Assembly constituency)") 4. [Fermanagh & South Tyrone CC](/wiki/Fermanagh_and_South_Tyrone_(Assembly_constituency) "Fermanagh and South Tyrone (Assembly constituency)") 8. [Lagan Valley CC](/wiki/Lagan_Valley_(Assembly_constituency) "Lagan Valley (Assembly constituency)") | [Parliamentary constituencies in Northern Ireland](/wiki/File:NorthernIrelandParliamentaryConstituenciesNumbered.svg "Parliamentary constituencies in Northern Ireland")  Parliamentary constituencies in Northern Ireland | 10. [Mid Ulster CC](/wiki/Mid_Ulster_(Assembly_constituency) "Mid Ulster (Assembly constituency)") 11. [Newry & Armagh CC](/wiki/Newry_and_Armagh_(Assembly_constituency) "Newry and Armagh (Assembly constituency)") 12. [North Antrim CC](/wiki/North_Antrim_(Assembly_constituency) "North Antrim (Assembly constituency)") 13. ## Scottish Parliament constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Scottish Parliament constituencies")]

Main article: [Scottish Parliament constituencies and electoral regions](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_constituencies_and_electoral_regions "Scottish Parliament constituencies and electoral regions")

[Scottish Parliament constituencies](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_constituencies "Scottish Parliament constituencies") are sometimes called *Holyrood* constituencies, to distinguish them from *Westminster* (House of Commons) constituencies.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The [Scottish Parliament Building](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_Building "Scottish Parliament Building") is in the [Holyrood](/wiki/Holyrood_(Edinburgh) "Holyrood (Edinburgh)") area of [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), while the main meeting place of the Parliament of the United Kingdom is the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), in the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

There are 73 Holyrood constituencies covering [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and each elects one [Member of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") (MSP) by the [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_system "First-past-the-post system"). Also, the constituencies are grouped into eight electoral regions, and each of these regions elects seven [additional members](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system"), to produce a form or degree of [mixed-member proportional representation](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The existing constituencies were created, effectively, for the [first general election](/wiki/1999_Scottish_Parliament_election "1999 Scottish Parliament election") of the Scottish Parliament, in 1999. When created, all but two had the names and boundaries of Westminster constituencies. The two exceptions were the [Orkney Holyrood constituency](/wiki/Orkney_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney (Scottish Parliament constituency)"), covering the [Orkney Islands council area](/wiki/Orkney_Islands_council_area "Orkney Islands council area"), and the [Shetland Holyrood constituency](/wiki/Shetland_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Shetland (Scottish Parliament constituency)"), covering the [Shetland Islands council area](/wiki/Shetland_Islands_council_area "Shetland Islands council area"). For Westminster elections, these [council areas](/wiki/Council_areas_of_Scotland "Council areas of Scotland") were covered (and still are covered) by the [Orkney and Shetland Westminster constituency](/wiki/Orkney_and_Shetland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney and Shetland (UK Parliament constituency)").

In 1999, under the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) the expectation was that there would be a permanent link between the boundaries of Holyrood constituencies and those of Westminster constituencies. This link was broken, however, by the [Scottish Parliament (Constituencies) Act 2004](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_(Constituencies)_Act_2004 "Scottish Parliament (Constituencies) Act 2004"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) which enabled the creation of a new set of Westminster constituencies without change to Holyrood constituencies. The new Westminster boundaries became effective for the [2005 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election").

## Senedd constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Senedd constituencies")]

Main article: [Senedd constituencies and electoral regions](/wiki/Senedd_constituencies_and_electoral_regions "Senedd constituencies and electoral regions")

There are 40 Senedd constituencies covering [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), and each elects one [Member of the Senedd](/wiki/Member_of_the_Senedd "Member of the Senedd") (MS) by the [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_system "First-past-the-post system"). Also, the constituencies are grouped into five electoral regions, and each of these regions elects four [additional members](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system"), to produce a form or degree of [mixed-member proportional representation](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation").

The current set of Senedd constituencies is the second to be created. The first was created for the [first general election](/wiki/1999_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "1999 National Assembly for Wales election") of the National Assembly for Wales, in 1999.

## European Parliament constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Kingdom_constituencies&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: European Parliament constituencies")]

Main article: [European Parliament constituencies in the United Kingdom](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituencies_in_the_United_Kingdom "European Parliament constituencies in the United Kingdom")

Before its [withdrawal from the European Union](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_the_European_Union "Withdrawal from the European Union") in 2020, the United Kingdom elected its [Members of the European Parliament (MEPs)](/wiki/Members_of_the_European_Parliament_(MEPs) "Members of the European Parliament (MEPs)") through twelve multimember [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") [constituencies](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituency "European Parliament constituency"). One, [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_(European_Parliament_constituency) "Northern Ireland (European Parliament constituency)"), used [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote"), while the eleven covering [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") used the [d'Hondt method](/wiki/D%27Hondt_method "D'Hondt method") of [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation").

For its first [European Parliamentary elections](/wiki/European_Parliamentary_elections "European Parliamentary elections") in [1979](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "1979 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") Great Britain was divided into a number of single-member [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") constituencies, matching the way Westminster MPs are elected. Following the decision that all MEPs should be elected by some form of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), the [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") government passed the [European Parliamentary Elections Act 1999](/wiki/European_Parliamentary_Elections_Act_1999 "European Parliamentary Elections Act 1999"), creating eleven constituencies in Great Britain, which were first used in [1999](/wiki/1999_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "1999 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The [South West England constituency](/wiki/South_West_England_(European_Parliament_constituency) "South West England (European Parliament constituency)") was expanded from the [2004 elections](/wiki/2004_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2004 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") onward to include [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"), the only [British overseas territory](/wiki/British_overseas_territory "British overseas territory") that was part of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), following a court case.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)
